dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported unconscious patient at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
My name is Ryan, I'm an EMT with Central Rescue. Did anyone call 911 about the patient found unresponsive?
Yes, my son was found unresponsive on the couch. I tried to wake him with water and slaps, but he didn't respond.
Hey, can you open your eyes for me? Can you hear me?
silence
No response - unresponsive with no eye movement or verbal response detected. Partner, check pupils for response.
Pupils are 1mm in both eyes and not reactive to light.
Agonal respirations are present. I'll place an oropharyngeal airway and begin with BVM.
OPA inserted. Let's assist ventilations now. Partner, prep for an IV.
IV placed 18g in the left arm. Pulse is 120, BP is 158 over 80.
Respiratory rate is 6 per minute. O2 saturation is 41%. Bagging immediately is necessary.
Ventilating now. I'll grab naloxone and prepare a dose.
Administering naloxone, 1 mg intranasally now.
Glucose is 519. Pulse remains high at 120.
Family reported the possibility of past drug use and some drinking the night before. Partner, document this as per protocols - then relay for protocol purposes.
He said he took a pill earlier but didn't specify what it was for.
Noted. Thanks for the update. We'll ask directly once he regains responsiveness.
No reports of pain observed or complained of.
Given hypoxia and suspected opioid use, we're treating for altered mental status, following protocol 3-15.
Respiratory rate at 6 per minute.
Saturations are 41%, still very low.
Blood pressure at 158 over 80.
Heart rate is 120.
Glucose remains at 519.
Symmetrical facial features. Pupils are now 3mm and reactive.
Skin is normal, warm, and dry with no rashes or trauma noted.
PMS intact throughout all extremities. No signs of swelling or deformity.
Clear bilateral breath sounds although not reported as a distress concern. No signs of JVD.
Administering a second naloxone dose now, 1 mg into the right nare.
Second dose placed. IV remains intact. Patient is showing signs of responsiveness.
Respiratory rate improved to 20 per minute.
Saturation up to 100%, significant improvement.
BP is 133/81 now.
Heart rate is down to 86.
Glucose remains at 519.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with no ectopy seen.
BVM remains effective. IV in good position. Naloxone showing clear benefit - patient is now AAOx4.
Second naloxone administered. He's talking now. EKG stable.
GCS of 15 recorded.
Respiratory rate now at 16 per minute.
Heart rate now at 82.
BP at 164 over 82.
Initial transport decision was refused by the patient, but the family convinced him to go to Chippenham Hospital.
Ready to move. Partner, prepare Reeves. Secure lines and pulse oximeter.
Rolling onto stretcher now. ABCs intact.
Transporting with vitals ongoing. Family - will you remain during transport?
I want to sit in the bench seat only, not on the stretcher.
Understood. We'll try that but must secure you safely for transport. Family, who will remain?
I will stay. No one else will go in.
Transport protocols are in place. Partner, call Chippenham Hospital for arrival.
Transferring a 37-year-old male with suspected opioid overdose and high glucose. Initial unresponsive with agonal respirations, improved post-naloxone.
IV intact in left arm. Vitals: HR 86, BP 133/81, SpO2 100%, RR 20. Second dose of naloxone just given before transport.
No chest or abdominal signs. 4-lead EKG showed sinus rhythm. No signs of respiratory distress confirmed.
Ready for movement. Let's secure the bench seat safely. Partner, direct EMS to admit room.
Moved and secured on bench seat. ABCs intact. Patient oriented and insists he doesn't need care.
Confirming it's safe to move. Partner, request assistance on arrival in the hospital.
Secure. Let's wheel it in.
Requesting the RN prepare for arrival - he prefers a bench seat in triage.
All items documented. No further interventions required at this time.
